incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. We want to try a bigger scale to reduce the number of tick marks.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 10 button highlights on selector panel.

steps.text: Look at the scale selector. We currently have scale of 5, which gives us 17 tick marks. Scale of 10 will cut that number in half. Click Scale of 10.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button three or more times. System models the correct action.

params.description: Scale of 10 button animates selection. Graph updates to scale of 10. Axis now shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80—only 9 tick marks.

steps.text: Let me show you. I click Scale of 10. The axis still goes from 0 to 80, but now the tick marks are 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80. That is 9 tick marks instead of 17. Bigger scales mean fewer tick marks. That is why the graph is easier to read.
